items.question: Define the likelihood function L(θ) and explain how it differs from a probability distribution over θ.
items.answer: L(θ) = f(x | θ) with observed data x fixed and θ varying. It scores parameter values by how well they explain the data. Unlike P(θ | x), it is not normalized over θ and is not generally a probability distribution over parameters.

items.question: Write the likelihood for n IID Bernoulli(p) trials with k successes. What is the MLE of p?
items.answer: L(p) = p^k (1−p)^{n−k} for p ∈ [0,1]. Maximizing gives MLE p̂ = k/n.

items.question: Why do we optimize log-likelihood instead of likelihood in ML?
items.answer: log is monotonic so argmax is unchanged; products become sums (easier derivatives, mini-batch averages); and log avoids numerical underflow when multiplying many small probabilities.

items.question: Relate cross-entropy to likelihood for multi-class classification.
items.answer: For true class y and predicted class probabilities π from softmax, −log π_y equals negative log-likelihood of a Categorical(π) model. Averaging over the dataset and minimizing is maximum likelihood (equivalently minimizing cross-entropy).

items.question: Where does likelihood appear in Bayes' theorem, and what role does the prior play?
items.answer: Bayes: P(θ | x) ∝ P(θ) · L(θ) where L(θ) = P(x | θ). The likelihood supplies evidence from data; the prior P(θ) regularizes which parameter values are plausible before seeing x.
